data.title: Intel Value Trap: The Potential 18A To 14A Shift In Foundry
data.description:
In our last Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) coverage, we noted our increasing wariness over Intel’s heightening revenue exposure in China despite intensifying geopolitical and regulatory headwinds. Our earlier concerns that the U.S. semiconductor export curbs, which kicked off in late 2022, could risk spilling over into PC chips currently dominating Intel’s sales are starting to materialize. The U.S. government has continued to prioritize export restrictions on advanced AI processors and related semiconductor technologies to China for now, and has yet to put a direct target on advanced PC chips supplied by Intel. But adjacent regulations, such as Taiwan’s recent addition of Huawei – a key Intel customer – to its version of the Entity List, are amplifying risks to Intel’s critical China market sales pipeline.

Meanwhile, the upcoming July 9 deadline on tariff decisions are also poised to levy a structural shift in Intel’s cost structure to the downside. Not only are semiconductors exposed to sectoral tariffs currently estimated at about 25%, but Intel’s significant China revenue mix also subjects it to risks of retaliatory tariff uncertainties given the precarious nature of U.S.-China trade relations.

In the latest development, Intel’s also been reportedly mulling the cancellation of external volumes of its foundry unit’s 18A process node, with intentions to skip straight to the more advanced 14A technology. The accelerated transition timeline appears to be an aggressive attempt to bolster Intel Foundry’s appeal to the likes of advanced chipmakers like Nvidia (NVDA) while secular AI demand remains strong. Although Intel has yet to provide direct confirmation on the matter, this piece of news shouldn’t be entirely dismissed in the stock’s investment decision-making process. It’s a stark reminder that Intel Foundry’s 18A node has yet to secure material external customer interest, which has been repeatedly acknowledged under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership.

While the Intel stock has continued to trade resiliently range-bound in the low-$20 levels, we believe its risk profile has become increasingly skewed to the downside. The mounting list of imminent macro, regulatory, trade policy, and company-specific challenges are poised to compound Intel’s existing plate of execution headwinds in leading its turnaround. Taken together, we remain incrementally cautious heading into Intel’s upcoming earnings update, as investors may be in for a rude awakening with evolving dynamics in the chipmaker’s operating backdrop likely setting the stage for further disappointment.

18A's Limited Resonation with Customers

While Intel Foundry initially started off as a key piece to Intel’s turnaround plan under previous CEO Pat Gelsinger’s IDM 2.0 strategy, it’s rapidly become a burden for the company’s fundamental outlook instead. Intel Foundry’s repeatedly been dogged by negative headwinds in recent years, spanning a potential spin-off and/or buyout from a rival to reports of outright rejection from potential customers due to its inferior performance. While some have been actively denied by the management of associated parties, others have been proven without response. For instance, Broadcom (AVGO) has yet to provide endorsement for Intel Foundry nor formally follow-up on earlier reports that it’s turned down 18A, citing the technology as “not yet viable to move to high-volume production” for its needs.

Despite limited interest from fabless chipmakers, Foundry’s scored some high-profile commitments for custom processors from hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT), nonetheless. Yet the lack of transparency on deal size and progress on this front remains a weak link for brewing doubt over the true traction of Foundry’s external roadmap.

Under Tan’s leadership, the first thing he’s repeatedly acknowledged is that there remains more work to be done to ensure the success of Intel Foundry, given 18A’s limited resonation with existing market demand. In the first order of business, Tan’s committed to spending more time engaging with existing and prospective foundry clients to better understand their needs and build trust:

Tan's vision centers on customer-centricity and engineering excellence. He pledges that Intel will “listen closely” to help address customer and partner needs. Moreover, he stresses the importance of embracing a more software-led design approach and designing purpose-built silicon to enable key workloads for customers. Embracing AI-driven system design, Intel will accelerate development of full-stack solutions and purpose-built silicon for specific outcomes, such as new architectures and critical workflows. This reflects Tan's aim to rebuild trust and strengthen ecosystem partnerships.

Source: Intel 2025 Vision Day Keynote Roundup.

…we need to build trust with foundry customers. We have a lot of important building blocks in place…However, I know from my years at Cadence Design that success in foundry business requires more than process technology manufacturing capabilities alone. It is first and foremost a customer service business, built on foundational principle of trust. And we need to instill customer service mindset across our foundry business. Success in foundry relies on recognizing that each customer use different design tools, methodologies, and styles.

Source: Intel Q1 '25 Earnings Call Transcript.

For now, 18A’s largest customer remains Intel Products itself – specifically the upcoming “Panther Lake” client CPU that begins shipping at year-end. This is consistent with management’s expectations that external 18A SKUs won’t be added to the mix until at least later 2026.

More critically, the lagging external adoption is clear confirmation that Intel Foundry’s investments continue to trail its goals of regaining node leadership. Recall Intel management, under Gelsinger’s leadership, had previously touted 18A as the world’s most advanced process node. Yet key rival TSMC (TSM) had, at the time, denied these claims. As we’d discussed in a previous coverage, TSMC management has clarified their belief that Intel 18A’s competitive advantage in performance and cost considerations over its N3P node may exist for Intel Products only:

Charlie Chan

I am very curious about your comments about the technology leadership, because your competitor knows all customer intel, states that their PPA is ahead of your 2-nanometer, even the cost is lower. So, I want to consult your opinion, why there's a different story and how do we judge...

C. C. Wei

…The comment stays the same, so that they are new is the technology would be very similar or equivalent to TSMC's N3P. We further check again with all the specs or the possible published in technology, transistor technology and everything. My comment stays the same with a big advantage in the technology maturity. Because of, in 2025, when they say that their newest technology will be go on production. For TSMC, that will be the third year with a very high-volume production in the fabs…

Mark Liu

Let me add some color to this. I think C. C. has been very modest. I think he's claimed that N3P is comparable to their 18A. We still affirm our statement. But I would like you to look at a different perspective. And what C. C., what the other side, the claim might be right, but it's only to their own product. And IDN typically their technology for their own product. We have foundry us, we optimize our technology for our customers' product. So, that's a big difference…

Source: TSMC Q4 '23 Earnings Call Transcript.

While the jury was still out at the time on whether 18A or TSMC’s N3 and N2 nodes are better, the question seems to have answered itself, given limited external endorsement for Intel Foundry today. What’s more pressing is that volume production of TSMC’s more competitive N2 node is just right around the corner, adding pressure to Intel Foundry’s roadmap. Specifically, TSMC’s N2 process node goes into production later this year, with confirmed commitments from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) for its next-generation EPYC Venice server CPUs, and Apple (AAPL) for its iPhone 18’s A20 processor. There’s even been reports that Intel itself might be committed to TSMC’s N2 node for the production of its upcoming Nova Lake client CPU, alongside dependence on its internal 18A capacity.

Implications of an Accelerated 14A Transition

Taken together, the reallocation of resources to ramp up external production capabilities on 14A would be a strategic option for Intel if that’s what customers ultimately want, and what’s needed for its foundry unit to maintain performance parity, if not superiority, against key rival TSMC.

We’ve already seen Intel deploy a similar strategy not too long ago. In one of Gelsinger’s final turnaround efforts made for Intel, he led his team in pulling the plug on 20A in favor of skipping directly to facilitating 18A’s go-to-market. The rationale at the time was that 20A’s primarily designed to facilitate internal production of the “Arrow Lake” client processor, which could be outsourced instead and save the company $500 million in unnecessary capex.

While the potential decision to skip external production on 18A would be a similar strategic decision as scrapping 20A, the fundamental implications would likely be entirely different. Unlike 20A, which was skipped before volume production began, 18A is already ramping up output to support Panther Lake’s go-to-market later this year. As a result, suspending 18A’s external production plans scheduled for 2026 would effectively mean forfeiting anticipated future revenues, which could trigger write-offs that the accelerated transition to 14A likely won’t be sufficient to fully offset.

Based on Intel’s latest 10K filing, the company’s disclosed it uses the cost method in valuing its long-lived assets under GAAP. What this means is that the initial measurement of assets – such as property, plant and equipment, and intangible assets – would be based on the total capitalizable cost of bringing it to operations (e.g., installation and development costs). And the value of its assets would be subject to depreciation on a straight-line basis over their estimated eight-year average useful life – which under GAAP is defined as the timeline over which the asset is determined to generate an economic benefit to the business. The value of its assets is also subject to an annual assessment to ensure the recoverable value (i.e., value of total cash flows the asset can generate over its useful life) remains higher than its cost basis. In the event the recoverable value of the asset falls below its cost basis, then it’d need to be impaired for the difference.

In Intel’s case, the potential decision to bypass 18A external volume production would essentially mean relinquishment of any future related revenues that the company had previously assigned to the project. While the exact asset valuation attributable to Intel’s 18A process node is not separately disclosed on the company’s balance sheet, it’s likely it’ll be subject to impairment in the event that external volume production on the technology is axed altogether in favor of a premature transition to 14A. For context, it’s estimated that Intel has allocated about $95 billion out of its total capex over the past four years in Foundry-related developments, underscoring the scale of investment exposed to risks of impairment in the event of an accelerated transition to 14A.

As a result, the potential node-to-node jump would be a strategic rather than financial-driven decision. The debate is likely focused on the trade-off between continuing the ramp-up of 18A capacity despite tepid external demand or accelerating to 14A in hopes of salvaging some existing chipmaker interest that rival capacity may be struggling to fulfil. The latter may prove to be a prudent decision, since Intel Foundry’s 14A node boasts more competitive performance against key rival TSMC’s existing N3 and upcoming N2 nodes. An accelerated transition to external volumes on 14A might just help Intel Foundry catch the last train in facilitating the production of next-generation advanced processors currently in development by fabless chipmakers like Nvidia and Apple as well.

More importantly, the fast-tracked undertaking could potentially prevent Intel Foundry from shipping into an impending slowdown – a costly mistake that the company’s been caught in time and again. For instance, repeated go-to-market delays for Intel’s Sapphire Rapids server CPU eventually led the chip to shipping into a cyclical slowdown in 2023. This caused Intel to essentially miss out on the bulk of the cloud spend era and lose significant revenue share in the server CPU market to AMD’s EPYC series processors.

A similar shortcoming was observed in Intel’s data center GPU strategy at the onset of generative AI momentum. Its only competitive AI processor was the Gaudi 3, which didn’t go to market until later 2024 – almost two years after the debut of OpenAI’s ChatGPT that triggered global interest in generative AI developments. By then, Nvidia’s already made two generational leaps in advanced AI chips – including the advancement from H100 to H200 on the Hopper architecture, and the subsequent debut of the Blackwell architecture backing B200. Similarly, AMD has also made significant strides, launching both the Instinct MI300 and MI325X series accelerators over the same period. Both of AMD and Nvidia’s products maintained superior performance over Intel’s Gaudi slate. This had eventually led management to acknowledge Gaudi’s underperformance, with a subsequent decision to pull the plug on the development of its successor, Falcon Shores, as they go back to the drawing table on Intel’s data center and AI roadmap.

As a result, the accelerated transition to 14A may even be a prescient strategy to fend imminent macro headwinds. The upcoming kick-off of reciprocal tariffs on July 9 will inevitably upend industry cost structures. Although AI spending on advanced hardware has been resilient due to secular tailwinds, they’re not entirely immune to belt-tightening decisions if the economy enters into a protracted downturn. This would be similar to cloud spend optimization trends that’ve emerged during the Fed’s latest monetary policy tightening cycle amidst runaway inflation in 2022, despite secular tailwinds for the technology at the time. The impending risk is further corroborated by the Fed’s decision to resume rate cuts later this year. An extended monetary easing cycle has typically coincided with an extended downturn, as seen last during the Global Financial Crisis in 2008. Taken together, Intel Foundry’s potential decision to fast-track external production ramp-up on 14A with subsequent go-to-market in 2026 could mark its first decisive step in correcting a series of past missteps, and drive real progress towards its turnaround efforts.

Fundamental and Price Considerations

The potential for an accelerated shift in Intel Foundry’s external production roadmap from 18A to 14A essentially harbingers incremental execution risks, given the pressed development timelines to meet the demands of upcoming advanced chip designs in the industry. This would compound impending tariff and regulatory headwinds, to which Intel remains inherently more sensitive to, given its significant China revenue exposure. Taken together, the overlapping challenges could further skew Intel’s risk profile to the downside.

Although we see partial strategic merit in the potential acceleration of 14A external volume production ahead of looming cyclical headwinds, it’d likely come at the expense of significant adverse implications on Intel’s near-term fundamentals. This would be unfavorable to neither its bottom line nor free cash flow trajectory, indicating potential requirements to further tap into external funding and/or risks of extended delays to capacity expansion plans critical for long-term growth.

Taken together, we believe Intel’s fundamental and valuation outlook is skewed to the downside scenario. In this case, our fundamental forecast projects Intel’s revenue to expand at a five-year CAGR of 7.0%. This growth trajectory will facilitate a return to GAAP income in 2026, with the figure to expand further at a four-year CAGR of 15.0% through 2030. This compares to our previously discussed base case projections that illustrate revenue expansion at a five-year CAGR of 10.3%, alongside a return to GAAP income by 2026 with further expansion at a four-year CAGR of 23.0% through 2030.

Our downside scenario price target for the stock is $16 per share, which is 28% below the stock’s currently traded price ($22.2 on July 3 open) and 16% below our base case price target of $19 per share.

The price is computed under a discounted cash flow analysis based on Intel’s downside scenario fundamental projections discussed above. Key assumptions include a 9.3% WACC, reflecting Intel’s capital structure and overall risk profile. We’ve also applied a 3.5% perpetual growth rate on projected 2029 EBITDA in projecting Intel’s terminal value. This assumption aligns with the expected long-term economic growth across Intel’s core operating markets, and reflects the pace at which its steady-state cash flows – proxied by 2029 EBITDA – can grow sustainably.

Risks to the Downside Scenario

Although we remain confident that Intel’s near-term fundamental outlook remains skewed to the downside as internal operating challenges mount alongside an unfavorable macro and policy backdrop, we acknowledge that its market valuation has already been subject to significant correction in the past several years. More importantly, the stock’s maintained resilience at the low-$20 level through the first half of calendar 2025 potentially suggests the worst has been priced in, with markets remaining optimistic in turnaround-driven upside from here on out.

This is corroborated by the Intel stock’s recently positive response to recent developments pertaining to the upcoming Republican tax and spending bill. Specifically, the bill, which is due for finalization on July 4, currently includes a raise in tax incentives for semiconductor expansion plans based in the U.S. to 35%, compared to the current 25%. Chipmakers looking to break ground on new facilities on U.S. soil by 2026 would be eligible for the benefit, which is stackable on top of previously announced CHIPS and Science Act grants.

And Intel is currently viewed as a primary beneficiary, given it’s already broken ground on the “Ohio One” facility, which includes two fabs – namely, Mod 1 and Mod 2 – in New Albany. However, the delayed start of production from later this year to 2030 poses risks to Intel’s eligibility to the increased tax incentive, which remains to be seen. Meanwhile, Intel Foundry’s Fab 52 and Fab 62 Arizona plants currently under construction are unlikely to benefit from the upcoming tax credit bump, since the new capacity’s set to come online later this year and likely won’t meet the criteria as a “new build” before 2026.

Meanwhile, even under prospects of imminent impairment costs if Intel Foundry proceeds with an accelerated transition timeline from18A to 14A, the stock has also continued to deliver nominal gains. This potentially suggests investors’ optimistic focus on the strategy’s long-term positives rather than the near-term escalation of execution risks ahead, despite the added pressure on Intel’s already fragile fundamentals. As a result, our downside scenario projections for Intel could prove onerous in the event the stock stays resilient enough to see through a successful turnaround in Intel’s operations across both its Products and Foundry units.

Final Thoughts

The potential pivot in Intel Foundry’s external volume roadmap from 18A to 14A could introduce deepening challenges to the company’s already difficult turnaround. While the move could prove strategically necessary, it adds further execution risks to Intel’s already fragile fundamental prospects.

Although the stock has maintained relative resilience this year, the underlying operating backdrop has continued to deteriorate, leaving it little room for missteps. Yet the impending kick-off of tariffs and new regulatory restrictions harbingers risk of unlocking an industry-wide shock. This could emulate the market selloff observed in April when President Trump introduced the preliminary list of reciprocal tariffs, which Intel likely remains comparatively more vulnerable to given its dominant China revenue exposure and overall fundamental fragility.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Is The Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF Lagging Peers?
data.description:
With Bitcoin (BTC-USD) getting increasingly gobbled up by the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT) and Bitcoin Treasury companies like Strategy (MSTR)(STRF), not every US-listed Bitcoin ETF has benefited from the same type of inflows as the market leader. In this article, we'll look at the Invesco Galaxy Bitcoin ETF (BATS:BTCO) relative to peers.

The fund's BTC holdings have fallen year to date while the broader US-based Bitcoin ETF segment has seen year to date inflows of over 21% measured in BTC. Is there a reason why the fund has lagged the rest of the market in AUM growth over the last 6 months? I think yes.

Bitcoin Capital Flows and Provider AUM

As of June 27th, the capital flow story for Bitcoin continues to be incredibly impressive in 2025. As of article submission, the digital asset sector is working on 11 consecutive weeks of positive net flows into investment products. Bitcoin's month to date investment product net flow as of June 27th was $4.6 billion. Year date net flow came in at nearly $15 million through the first half of 2025:

Asset (mil$) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin $4,604 $14,925 $159,399
Ethereum (ETH-USD) $1,432 $2,859 $14,614
Multi-asset -$17.3 $58 $6,814
Solana (SOL-USD) $8.3 $91 $1,328
XRP (XRP-USD) $21.2 $219 $1,184
Total $6,059 $17,776 $184,414
BTC Dominance 76.0% 84.0% 86.4%

Bitcoin is clearly benefiting from demand through capital flows with 76% of digital asset net flow month to date and 84% share year to date. These are impressive numbers. It isn't just a Bitcoin story though, this is very much a BlackRock (BLK) story as well. When we break down the capital flows by provider, it's quite clear there is a dominant product provider in the market. The iShares funds account for 77% of the month to date flows and a staggering 96% of digital asset investment demand year to date.

Provider (mil$) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
iShares $4,651 $17,053 $78,605
Grayscale $74 -$1,652 $29,892
Fidelity $355 $249 $21,516
ARK 21 Shrs $97 $75 $5,018
Bitwise $227 -$59 $4,511
ProShares $90 $526 $4,101
21Shares AG $18 -$200 $3,750
CoinShares XBT -$57 -$269 $3,446
Other $605 $2,055 $33,575
Total $6,059 $17,776 $184,414

Source: CoinShares/Bloomberg, as of June 27th, 2025

Invesco's share of the digital asset investment space is so small that CoinShares doesn't even break it out in the company's digital asset fund flow reports. When we assess BTC AUM as a percent of total issuer AUM, Invesco's Bitcoin holdings are a rounding error while iShares has 1% of total AUM in Bitcoin:

Billions (as of Q1-25) Total AUM Bitcoin ETF BTC %
Invesco $1,845 $0.44 0.02%
iShares $4,303 $46.60 1.08%

Sources: Invesco, YCharts

This kind of dominance is hard to overcome if you're a competitor like Invesco and it begs the obvious question; why?

BTCO Fund Details

Like the other spot Bitcoin ETFs listed in the United States, BTCO launched in mid-January 2024. For the sake of comparison, the table below shows fund details for the following Bitcoin ETFs:

  • WisdomTree Bitcoin Fund ETF (BTCW)
  • Franklin Bitcoin ETF (EZBC)
  • CoinShares Valkyrie Bitcoin ETF (BRRR)
  • Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB)
  • VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL)

What each of these funds have in common is that they are the bottom 6 US-listed Bitcoin ETFs in the market in terms of assets under management:

Fund Details BTCO BTCW EZBC BRRR BITB HODL
Issuer Invesco WisdomTree Franklin Templeton Investments Valkyrie Digital Assets LLC Bitwise Investments VanEck
Expense Ratio 0.25% 0.25% 0.19% 0.00% 0.20% 0.00%
AUM $569.35M $169.44M $564.43M $641.00M $4.30B $1.72B

Source: Seeking Alpha

For me, the biggest standout difference between these funds is the expense ratios. At 0.25%, BTCO is on the expensive side relative to the selected funds above. But by itself, that doesn't necessarily explain the lack of interest in BTCO. For instance, BITB appears to be a significant outlier in this table. At a 0.20% expense ratio, that fund is closer to BTCO than a fund like BRRR yet has the largest AUM in our sample by far. In my view, there are two key reasons for this.

First, Bitwise Investments is a crypto-native asset manager. This is not an issuer that touches traditional index ETFs and even has a digital asset fund that offers exposure to tokens like Solana (SOL-USD) and Litecoin (LTC-USD). Thus, I think Bitwise has benefited from crypto trustworthiness to a larger degree than each of the other issuers shows. Also, BITB uses part of its management fee to fund Bitcoin development. The other issuer that has committed to such an action is VanEck - so it's likely not surprising to see each of these two funds with the two largest AUMs of the bottom 6 funds.

Still, this doesn't explain why BTCO has performed so badly against a fund like IBIT. I suspect the original roll out of these funds has been a factor. Consider this screenshot from my Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin ETF (FBTC) from January 2024:

Back in January 2024 when these funds originally launched, BTCO would have had one of the higher post-waiver fees in the market at 0.39%. Even though there was a 0.00% waiver on the first $1 billion in AUM or for 6 months, investors looking for long term solutions likely balked at BTCO due to the fee after waiver. BRRR also had a high post-waiver fee and has a very similar AUM to that of BTCO today.

Fund Performance

Ultimately, what likely matters most for potential BTCO investors is how the fund actually performs compared to the products in the market. So far, there is virtually no difference between these funds based on total return over the last year:

We can see the Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC) is the obvious laggard of the bunch above. That is entirely due to the fund's egregious 1.5% expense ratio. But short of choosing to minimize third party custodian risk to Coinbase (COIN) through FBTC, there is very little difference between these products.

Closing Thoughts

For investors looking for a simple way to get Bitcoin exposure through the ETF wrapper, BTCO will work perfectly fine. Is it the cheapest fund? No. Is it the biggest by AUM? No. In fact, it's one of the smallest by AUM and that is likely due to some combination of the fund's current 0.25% expense ratio relative to slightly cheaper alternatives today and the fact that the product launched with one of the higher post-waiver fees in the market. The lack of early adoption is perhaps haunting AUM growth today. But, again, purely as a Bitcoin proxy, it'll likely work just as well as the other funds that rely on Coinbase for custody.

data.title: Equity Markets Are Looking Past Risk, But What About The Bond Market?
data.description:
Equity markets have largely looked past risks recently, but what about the bond market? MoneyTalk discusses what’s ahead with Alexandra Gorewicz, VP & Director, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management, TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Equity markets have largely looked past a variety of risks out there, but what's happening in the bond space? Joining us now to discuss, Alex Gorewicz, VP and Director for Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management.

Great to have you back on the program, Alex.

Alex Gorewicz: Thanks, Greg. Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell: We're at the halfway point of the year. We've entered the second half of the year. We have equity markets at all-time highs, no shortage of risk. The equities don't seem to mind. Do the bonds mind? What's going on bonds?

Alex Gorewicz: Not doing very much. Interest rates have been pretty range-bound of late. And I think there are a lot of forces that are pushing and pulling on where interest rates should go from here. So that's actually led to some surprisingly calm trading days in the bond market in the last couple of weeks.

Greg Bonnell: In the last couple of weeks? So we don't even have to go far back to have geopolitical risk. The war in the Middle East. We've had concerns over the US Big Beautiful Bill, what it could mean for the economy. There's a whole bunch of things that, I guess, in regular times, I would think the bond market would react to. What do you think is going on there that the bond market is seeing through these things?

Alex Gorewicz: I think each one of the factors that you've mentioned that historically could have or should have led to much bigger moves in the bond market, or a clear direction being set in the bond market, can actually be seen from multiple points of view.

So, for example, take immigration. This is a relatively easy one because-- well, there's nothing actually easy about it when you think about the pass-through effects to the job market. But when you take immigration policy on its own, a tighter labor market should mean stickier wages that could potentially push up on inflation, that should push up interest rates.

But then longer term, a smaller labor market generally means shrinking economy in the longer run. So all things being equal, those two factors are pushing and pulling on where interest rates should go from here.

I could do the same thing with the budget bill. For example, what's being currently pushed through the House is a substantially higher deficit than the initial move from the House to the Senate with the bill. And an extra half a billion-- oh, sorry, $500 billion to $600 billion of debt that needs to be factored in over the long term, that should push interest rates higher.

On the flip side, we're seeing a lot of commentary from the Treasury saying that they're going to fund a lot of that with Treasury bills. That doesn't mean an increase in bond supply, and that's weighing lower on interest rates.

So you see how all the different factors, all the different events and developments that you've highlighted, each one of them could be analyzed from different points of view and are having this offset, almost balanced impact on how interest rates are trading right now.

Greg Bonnell: From what we keep hearing from Jerome Powell in terms of patience, and this factor could be this but this factor could be that, slowing economy, maybe higher inflation-- they're on pause because they really don't know what to do in this situation. If you're taking a look-- we'll start breaking down the central banks.

But we'll start with the Fed because that's the one the market cares about the most, for the most part. What do we think is going on there? When do we think they might actually get back off the sidelines? What do they need to see?

Alex Gorewicz: So, I think every investor is going to take a slightly different view of the Fed reaction function. In my opinion, labor will matter most. I think if you have negative surprises-- and we had a negative surprise in ADP today-- but if you have a negative surprise, for example, on non-farm tomorrow, I think it increases the likelihood that the July meeting for the Fed is a live one. In other words, that they could very well cut interest rates, which is not fully priced by the market.

But if we go beyond just the next meeting, right now to the end of the year the bond market has two to three rate cuts priced into the Fed. I don't think that there's a lot of consensus around that number. And what I mean by that is, I think that just falling out of the distribution of how investors view what the Fed is going to do-- which is either they're going to be on hold for the rest of the year, or there are a lot more rate cuts that are coming.

And in terms of how I would put myself in either of those camps, I think it just depends on jobs. So if jobs are weak, if they surprise to the downside, I think you're more likely to see more rate cuts than what the bond market has currently priced.

Greg Bonnell: The other part of the equation for the Fed, and obviously the mandate in Canada too, is inflation. And I think there's been, after April 2, even heading up to April 2, all the threat of tariffs and then the big chart of the tariffs-- people said, inflationary, inflationary. It doesn't appear to be showing up in the numbers. What's going on there?

Alex Gorewicz: Correct. I think up to now you've had this Goldilocks outcome for the Fed. And what do I mean by that is jobs numbers have been more resilient than expected by the bond market, by economists, even probably, by the Fed themselves. And then inflation has been more benign or weaker than expected-- exactly like you've alluded to-- than the Fed has expected, economists had expected, investors had expected. So you've had this very positive outcome where jobs are great and inflation is not a problem.

Now we might be at that apex where it's going to go in bad directions on both fronts. What I mean by that is inflation could actually start to tick up from here, and jobs could actually fall from here, or it gets worse in the labor market.

What does the Fed do in that environment? And this is where, I think, there are different investors that have different points of view on what would happen. I believe the Fed's going to care more about the labor weakening, if it does.

Greg Bonnell: How about in Canada? What's going on here? Because, of course, the Fed has a dual mandate. The Bank of Canada is just supposed to be watching inflation, but they've been cautious and patient as well.

Alex Gorewicz: Correct. And I think in Canada you've seen more of that tariff impact on inflation already come through, versus the US, where it seems businesses and the business community is kicking the can down the road on passing tariffs through to consumers. You've seen that actually start to happen in Canada.

Greg Bonnell: And as a result, Bank of Canada is on pause, right? They're taking a much more cautious wait-and-see approach to see, is this transitory? Will it get worse from here? Will it affect more goods and services?

Alex Gorewicz: On the flip side, we've seen manufacturing employment be really weak for several months now in Canada. That's not a surprise. We know the business community has said there's so much uncertainty around tariffs and our trade negotiations with the US. I think if we get more clarity in the next month, the government, our government, has a self-imposed deadline--

Greg Bonnell: July 21st--

Alex Gorewicz: That's right.

Greg Bonnell: --I think, to try to make a deal.

Alex Gorewicz: I was going to say at the end of this month, roughly. But yes. If we get more clarity, I think there are enough green shoots in the Canadian economy that we can get through this soft patch.

What I worry about is if there is going to be persistent uncertainty for Canada and for the business community, I worry that the weakness we're seeing in manufacturing could spill over to the services sector and just the broader economy-- and that's obviously not going to be a good thing.

Right now, from the bond market's point of view, they think Bank of Canada maybe has one more rate cut in them, or maybe they just stay on pause for the remainder of the year. And again, it depends on how things evolve from here, and how much clarity we get around the nature of our economic relationship with the US going forward.

But if things persist, if the uncertainty persists, I'm in the camp that Bank of Canada should cut a lot more.

Greg Bonnell: Quickly, I'll ask you-- that's where we are and where we might be headed in the next little while. We've got six months ahead of us now for the rest of 2025. What are you watching?

Alex Gorewicz: So aside from the labor market, especially in the US-- although, again, also in Canada as it pertains to the services sector-- aside from labor, what I'm watching is what business leaders are saying. And right now, a lot of the sentiment is weak for the second half of the year. I'd like to see if that negative sentiment persists or if we start seeing a rebound.

Original Post

data.title: DSU Can Be An Opportunistic Strategy During Periods Of Economic Uncertainty
data.description:
The BlackRock Debt Strategies Fund (NYSE:DSU) is a closed-end fund [CEF] designed to provide investors with current income and exposure to a diversified portfolio corporate debt and loans. DSU pays out a forward rate of $1.18/share, yielding 11.15%.

DSU primarily invests in issuances that are rated BBB and lower, making this a high-yield strategy. 53.73% of the portfolio is made up of B-rated bonds; 28.54% of the portfolio’s issuances are BB-rated, and roughly 12% are CCC-rated or below, including not rated.

DSU is well diversified, spreading the risk across a total of 1,250 individual issuances. The weighted average life to maturity for the portfolio is 6.11 years, providing medium-term exposure in the corporate debt market. The average yield to worst across the portfolio is 9.68%, suggesting substantial upside potential for the held issuances.

The portfolio strategy primarily invests in US bonds and loans with little international exposure. 90% of the total weight derives from US issuances, nearly 4% in Canada, 2.34% in Luxemburg, and 1.14% in the UK.

Term loans make up the majority of the weight in DSU, commanding 76.32% of the total portfolio weight. High-yield bonds in the industrials sector is the second largest weighting at 13.72%. Given the growth potential of the industrials sector, particularly with nearshoring & reshoring, I suspect this strategy could be beneficial to DSU investors.

DSU pays out a monthly distribution with an annualized forward rate of $1.18/share, yielding 11.15%. Though the historical payout has varied from year to year and in some instances reduced, DSU has increased its distribution rate multiple times throughout the last 3 years.

Investors should be aware that distributions can be categorized across a variety of streams with the most recent DSU distributions being a return of capital. I recommend investors consult their financial planner prior to making a final investment decision to fully understand the potential tax implications.

DSU charges a relatively appealing fee of 1.24% considering the fees from similar funds. In terms of depth and liquidity, DSU has $536mm in assets under management with 244k shares valued at $2.6mm trading hands on a daily basis, providing adequate liquidity for investors seeking to enter or exit a position.

DSU has performed exceptionally well during long time frames, outperforming the majority of its peers on a 5-year and 10-year basis.

Taking performance into consideration, investors must recognize that high-yield bonds don’t offer the same stability as investment-grade bonds. High-yield bonds oftentimes perform similar to equities given the broader risk implications.

From a high level, the differentiator between high-yield and investment-grade issuances is the credit quality and operational performance of the organization. High-yield debt is typically issued by companies that may not be in the best financial position, potentially exhibiting signs of flagging growth, declining margins, or higher levels of leverage. In general, interest payments may hold a higher weight in terms of profitability for these companies, potentially posing the risk of solvency during times of economic stress and uncertainty.

For example, international trade disruptions, like the one that resulted from the imposed tariffs by President Trump, may result in increased costs to these organizations, potentially impacting the bottom line more severely when compared to investment-grade organizations. July 9, 2025 is the deadline for the tariff pause, bringing forth a wave of appealing trade deals or the return of heightened import duties. Though I suspect many trading partners will come forth with a win-win agreement with the US at the risk of lost business, there may be certain partners that do not come to an agreement, potentially resulting in Liberation Day-style rates.

Uncertainty, when looked through an optimistic lens, can create opportunity for high-yield issuances, as suggested by the sharp value decline in DSU experienced in April 2025. Though the risk of default may be relatively higher as a result of the additional costs from import duties, the portfolio should be diversified to the point of any single default being relatively immaterial to the broader strategy. For this reason, I believe the economic uncertainty has created a substantial opportunity for investors.

Interest rate policy may also impact high-yield bond performance. The general consensus on Wall Street is that the Fed may cut the key rate by 75-100bps in 2025. This could have two impactful implications to high-yield issuances. The first is that the issuances will become more appealing relative to the risk-free rate, potentially driving up the value of these bonds. The second is that the cost to refinance debt may be coming down, potentially positively impacting organizations that may not have the capital or cash flow to pay down maturing debt.

Overall, I believe that this high-yield strategy could perform well given the current economic environment. Though there are many factors at play that could derail the strategy, such as the deterioration of the international trade environment paired with high rates, I do not believe this level of disconnect between President Trump and Fed President Jerome Powell will occur. I am recommending DSU with a BUY rating with a target allocation in the range of 8-12% with the assumption of a mixed portfolio of equities, high-yield & investment-grade bond exposure.

data.title: O-I Glass: A Big Transformation Is Beginning
data.description:
Back in January of this year, one company that I decided to upgrade from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy’ was O-I Glass (NYSE:OI). If you are new to this business, it is worth mentioning that it focuses on the production and sale of glass containers that are used for alcoholic beverages, soft drinks, certain food products, juices, teas, and pharmaceutical products. This is what I have historically referred to as a ‘simple’ business because, operationally speaking, it's not complex at all. It's easy to understand and demand for it will almost certainly always exist.

I made the argument that the plunge that shares had experienced leading up to that point made it very attractively priced. And as such, I claimed that the stock deserved significant upside, especially if we assume that management will come through on their promises for the future. Admittedly, recent financial performance has been slightly negative. But on the whole, I found this enterprise to be too cheap to pass up. Given these factors, I believed then that the stock deserved a ‘strong buy’ rating. And in all honesty, I feel the exact same way now. This is in spite of the fact that the stock has risen 38.5% since that article, easily surpassing the 1.8% rise that the S&P 500 enjoyed.

Shares still offer upside

Recently, the fundamental picture for O-I Glass has been mixed, to slightly negative. Take the most recent data that we have as an example. This would cover the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue was $1.57 billion. That's a hair below the $1.59 billion the business reported one year earlier. This decline occurred in spite of the fact that the company saw revenue in its America's business grow from $854 million to $873 million. That rise, according to management, was thanks to higher selling prices that benefited revenue to the tune of $30 million. Management chalked this up to its ability to pass through higher inflationary costs on to its customers. Total glass container shipments for the region were actually up 4% as well. So clearly, the market was able to absorb these higher prices.

Internationally, the picture was different. In Europe, overall sales contracted from $709 million up to $667 million. Lower average selling prices hit revenue to the tune of $46 million. The good news is that we did see a rise in shipments amounting to 4% year over year. That helped to boost sales to the tune of $18 million. Unfortunately, that was not enough to offset the lower selling prices and the fact that foreign currency fluctuations negatively affected revenue to the tune of $14 million.

On the bottom line, the picture for the company worsened quite a bit. Driven by inflationary pressures, the firm reported a drop in net income from $72 million to negative $16 million. Another big pain point involved $82 million of restructuring, asset impairment, and other similar charges. Most of this involved the firm’s operations in Europe. But there were certain corporate costs and costs that were incurred here at home. As I mentioned in my previous article, management has been working hard on reinventing the business.

This has been part of its Fit to Win program, which is a major initiative aimed at reducing enterprise costs and at optimizing things like the firm's network and value chain. Specifically, this falls under its Horizon 1 push that began in 2024. However, the company does have two other phases of this plan that will begin in the future. Horizon 2 will emphasize leveraging the company's competitive position to drive future profitable growth. And Horizon 3 will see the company grow through geographic expansion, joint ventures, partnerships, and mergers and acquisitions activities. These are expected to begin in 2026 and 2028, respectively. So far, management has incurred $283 million of cumulative charges under the Fit to Win program. And in future quarters, the firm anticipates these costs to grow.

Other profitability metrics for the company have decreased as well. Operating cash flow is the one exception, going from negative $270 million to negative $171. If we adjust for changes in working capital, however, we would get a drop from $216 million to $197 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business contracted from $317 million to $294 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results from 2023 to 2024.

The drop in revenue here was sharp, Driven by not only lower pricing, but by a plunge in sales volumes. Management blamed all of this pain primarily on inventory destocking. In essence, it appears as though customer inventories had grown too much over the previous years, likely in response to supply chain issues that caused a ramping up of inventories. So this is just a return to more normal levels. Naturally, this would cause worsening in profitability also.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management believes that EBITDA will come in at between $1.15 billion and $1.20 billion. A similar year over year growth rate for adjusted operating cash flow would give us a reading of $655 million. But that's not all. As I mentioned already, management has a plan to optimize operations and to create shareholder value. By 2027, the firm expects to generate around $1.45 billion worth of EBITDA. And by 2029, they are targeting $1.65 billion. Assuming that adjusted operating cash flow will rise at the same rate, we would get readings of $808 million and $919 million, respectively.

Another thing to take into consideration here is that management intends to reduce the net leverage ratio for the company to 2.5 by 2027. And long term, they want to keep it between 2 and 2.5. This would mean a reduction in net debt from $4.59 billion today to $3.63 billion in 2027. For 2029, hitting the midpoint of what management is targeting would mean net debt of $3.71 billion. Incorporating this into the equation gives us the ability to figure out how cheap shares are on a forward basis for 2027 and 2029 if management can hit their objectives.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using these estimates and using historical results for 2024. These are already incredibly low multiples. In fact, in the table below, you can see how the company stacks up against five similar firms using the 2024 figures. In each case, O-I Glass is cheaper than the competition. And with only one exception, I would describe it as being drastically cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
O-I Glass 3.9 6.3
Ardagh Metal Packaging SA (AMBP) 4.0 10.3
TriMas (TRS) 15.2 12.6
Myers Industries (MYE) 7.9 9.9
Greif (GEF) 10.2 8.7
Silgan Holdings (SLGN) 9.9 12.3

I think a good argument here could be made for multiple expansion, especially based on management's plans. But even if that does not come to pass, the stock deserves some nice upside. If we assume that the firm does hit its targets for 2027 and 2029, and if we also assume that it continues to trade at the forward multiples estimated for it for 2025, we can see in the chart below what the annualized upside for shareholders would be. In the worst case scenario, we would be looking at upside of about 10.5% per annum. That's roughly what the market averages per year. But in other cases, especially when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, upside could be drastically higher. In this case, we would be looking at upside of 61.6% annually between now and the end of 2027 and 30.1% from now through the end of 2029. I would actually put greater credibility into the EV to EBITDA approach over the price to adjusted operating cash flow approach because of the plan to reduce overall leverage. And honestly, even if we cut these numbers in half, they would still represent market beating potential.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I think that the market continues to underestimate O-I Glass. The company looks incredibly cheap right now and I am of the professional opinion that changes being made by the company should help to instill additional confidence from the investment community into the business. Even if the firm continues to trade at the multiples that it is currently trading for, hitting its objectives for profit growth over the next few years would result in market matching returns at worst and market beating returns at best. This favorable risk to reward situation makes me feel confident in maintaining the firm as a ‘strong buy’ candidate.

data.title: Tesla's Q2 Deliveries: Inevitable Decline Slows, U.S. And Western Deliveries Rise
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) released delivery numbers for the second quarter of 2025 (Q2 2025) on the 2nd of July. Despite analysts' consensus deeming it a "miss," the stock price rose and showed mildly bullish trends as of the 3rd of July.

The underlying trends in deliveries are complicated yet encouraging - at least from some prime market movers' perspective.

Trend Drilldown

In the U.S., it is an unassailable fact that, while Tesla holds a commanding lead over all other carmakers, its market share has been shrinking for almost five years now.

The most significant competitors chipping away at Tesla's market share are fellow American carmakers Ford Motor Company (F) and General Motors Company (GM), with overseas carmakers Hyundai Motor Company (OTCPK:HYMTF) and Volkswagen AG (OTCPK:VWAGY, OTCPK:VLKAF, OTCPK:VWAPY) also steadily surging ahead. Various other legacy carmakers have products now that have been steadily chipping away at the higher end of Tesla's catalogue.

After witnessing explosive growth for over three years - 2021 through 2023 - the EV market is cooling off. The lack of widespread and universal charging architecture (particularly in non-coastal regions), the high cost of EVs relative to Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)-powered cars and a stubbornly persistent affordability crisis among Americans have all been contributory factors in this scenario. Electric Vehicles (EVs) are estimated to have constituted 8% of all new vehicles sold in 2024 while hybrids made up about twice that figure. As of Q1 2025, the market is trending towards a net 8% decline in sales for the full year. Early trends show hybrids essentially continuing to show a modest uptick relative to EVs in the U.S. market.

Note: Sales data from all carmakers haven't been made available as yet to include in the trend analysis.

After largely keeping up with the growth of EV sales in 2021, Tesla progressively fell behind until it witnessed a net negative growth in sales in 2024 while the market continued to register positive growth. Q2 witnessed a net change in - to coin a phrase - the "overall heaviness" in Tesla's gloom.

Tesla Delivery Trends

By the end of 2024, Tesla's main revenue driver - the relatively cheaper Model 3/Y platform - had fallen from 97% production share in 2021 to a generally persistent 95% in 2025. In the first half of 2025 (H1 2025), this had risen back to 97%.

Tesla's plants in the U.S. and Europe mostly service the Western Hemisphere while its plant is in Shanghai (China). While Tesla doesn't explicitly break down production or deliveries by plant/region, one trend that remains prevalent is that, over the mid- to long-run, production and delivery numbers don't significantly diverge. In other words, inventories tend to run pretty low and the mapping of regional demand by platform is quite efficient.

Between "China+" (signifying China retail sales and China-origin exports together) and ex-China facilities, deliveries had typically run close to 1.1-1.2 to 1. As of H1 2025, ex-China deliveries outpace "China+" deliveries by 0.35%. From 84% of U.S. consumption of its ex-China deliveries, it is estimated that this had slipped down to 78% in Q1 2025. Given that Giga Berlin - which only produces the cheaper Model Y (along with drivetrains and other subsystems) - had delivered its 500,000th EV by the end of March this year, this drop could be construed as the ex-China facilities growing in strength and contribution.

On the other hand, the China retail (i.e., domestic sales) composition of its "China+" facility had risen from 62% in 2022 to 78% in Q1 2025, which implies that exports are flagging and indicates rising competition: while Tesla might have a "homegrown innovator" advantage for consumers in the U.S., this doesn't apply in other car markets. Falling sales volumes make this more and more apparent.

However, there is one stark standout that stands out: as of Q1 2025, ex-China deliveries were trending at a 28% decline relative to 2024 while China retail was trending at a 20% decline. With Q2 2025 registering a 17% uptick in ex-China deliveries over Q1 2025, ex-China deliveries are now trending at an 18% decline as of H1 2025. Given Mr. Elon Musk's close association with the Trump administration (until a rather spectacular end very recently), there have been strident campaigns against buying Teslas both within the United States as well as the rest of the Western Hemisphere along with bouts of violence against both Tesla owners and dealerships. The near-halving of the decline trend in 2025 here, despite a plethora of options available to buyers, indicates that this might not have been successful. In fact, it might have had the exact opposite effect.

Maybe "cancel culture" has been cancelled?

Opportunities and Hurdles

Official China retail numbers - that effectively separate out exports from domestic sales - aren't available yet but "China+" deliveries are trending to be down 22% from 2024, wherein it delivered over 916,000 vehicles. In the month of June, China+ deliveries effectively reversed eight straight months of year-on-year decline to register a small uptick. With near-parity in current delivery/production trends between East and the West in 2025 and slightly lesser underperformance in the West despite the opposition (both from politics and the market), this might be a bottom from which the company either rises or doesn't fall quite as much as prognosticated.

However, it is a fact that - despite a trending decline in the U.S. EV market - Tesla's relative lack of freshness in delivering fresh new models to pique consumer interest means that the company has much to lose while competing brands entering the market with relatively newer models have much to gain. These competing brands have strong performance in other markets in Europe as well as China (along with proximate regions).

Tesla's decision to veer into autonomous driving is a potentially rich new vein to tap into, given the perhaps inevitable continued loss of market share. Some estimates for its nearest U.S. rival - Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo - assigns a price tag of nearly $250,000 for a car to be made fully autonomous. The initial Robotaxi launch in Austin (Texas) utilizes the existing Model Y with little by way of additional hardware. If this is the template that will be followed for full-scale rollout, there's potentially anywhere between a 300% to 400% price advantage over the existing competition. For potential Robotaxi fleet operators, this is a massive incentive.

On the other hand, the aforementioned "spectacular end" to CEO Musk's "buddy" relationship with the Trump administration over the provisions of President Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" (actual official name) has raised a worrying prospect to some: a threat to end an estimated $38 billion (until date) in subsidies to Musk's companies. Mr. Musk replied, "I am literally saying CUT IT ALL. Now." (presumably meaning all subsidies being given out by the government).

While the aforementioned Bill axes EV tax credits, it also currently has a provision that lets car buyers write off up to $10,000 a year in interest paid on qualifying auto loans taken out between 2025 and 2028 for the purchase of cars assembled in the U.S. Tesla is a decidedly American carmaker with facilities and only a small portion of buyers can be assumed to be buying Teslas in cash. This means that Tesla buyers stand to benefit in savings even if EV tax credits are erased.

Be it Musk or Tesla, there is plenty to pooh-pooh for some - perhaps even justifiably sometimes. But, nonetheless, Tesla's earnings call on the 23rd of July would likely be a very interesting one. If there are updates about Robotaxi deployments and/or a fresh new model, this would likely be yet another bullish signal.

While some might see the facts presented as net positives (as the stock's present conviction seems to indicate), there are also numerous factors riding against it. The automotive industry has never been a sprint; it's a marathon. And Tesla - like its boss - remains oh-so-interesting.

On the balance of all the facts, a Hold is in order.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2025 Midyear Investment Outlook - Getting A Grip On Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

Twice a year, BlackRock’s senior portfolio managers and investment executives gather for two days to debate the outlook for the global economy and markets – and its implications for portfolios. Since the previous Forum in November, we have met even more frequently to take stock of this year’s policy shifts driving financial market volatility – and elevated uncertainty. The Global Outlook is the culmination of that debate and discussion at our latest Forum and informs our macro framing: We have long argued this is a world being shaped by supply and undergoing a transformation driven by mega forces, or structural changes driving returns now and in the future. We evolve that by describing how we see the world losing long-term macro anchors that have underpinned investment processes for decades.

Our three themes spell out the investment implications of this. Investing for the here and now is about how we can have more certainty about the near term than long term – and why we stay pro-risk. Taking risk with no macro anchor highlights how macro risk needs to be managed in this environment – yet it is one of ample alpha opportunities. And finding anchors in mega forces highlights the important role of mega forces, such as artificial intelligence, in shaping growth trajectories and serving as a durable return driver.

With the many twists and turns over the first half of this year, policy uncertainty is obviously elevated. We had expected policy this year to switch from a stabilizing force for financial markets to a destabilizing force. That has played out. Yet at this midyear point, risk asset returns are also little changed so far, even with all the headline-grabbing noise.

What matters now for investors is getting a grip on this environment’s defining features. We have long argued that we entered a new macro regime marked by profound transformations, shaped by mega forces that could lead to many very different potential outcomes over time – for the trajectory and makeup of the global economy, inflation, government debt and deficits or global trade. 2025 has put this new regime into sharper relief, with serious discussion about the potential for fundamental changes to the structure of global markets – including the haven role of U.S. Treasuries or the U.S. dollar as a reserve currency.

Put another way, the loss of long-term macro anchors that have underpinned long-term asset allocation for decades is a defining feature of this new regime.

But the global economy can’t be revamped overnight. Immutable economic laws – on global trade and debt financing – exist that policy cannot ignore in the near term. Attempts to break them are akin to trying to break laws of physics – and defy gravity – in our view.

Sharp U.S. policy shifts and elevated uncertainty make it seem the world is upended. But this is an evolution of the new regime of transformation we have been in for a few years. What matters: getting a grip on uncertainty by identifying its core features. Long-term macro anchors are weaker given the many different potential outcomes. Yet macro fundamentals haven’t changed much for now: Immutable economic laws prevent policy from revamping the world overnight.

That keeps us positive on risk assets and U.S.stocks. Megaforces, like artificial intelligence, are the new long-term anchor for achieving durable returns.

Nobody knows where the macro environment is ultimately headed. But understanding these policy limits has allowed us to be nimble and build the conviction to dial risk back up just one week after the April 2 tariff announcements. It makes us more comfortable staying pro-risk on a tactical horizon. We have more certainty about the near-term macro outlook than the long term – an unusual situation for investors. So we put greater weight on tactical views. That’s why our first theme is investing in the here and now. That favors U.S. equities and themes such as artificial intelligence. We stand ready to pivot depending on the ultimate impact of U.S. policy on the economy. We don’t think Europe can outperform yet without structural changes – but some of the steps Europe has taken give us optimism.

Taking risk with no macro anchor is our second theme. We believe this environment of transformation is better than the prior decade for achieving above-benchmark returns, or alpha. Yet the volatile macro environment injects risk into portfolios that needs to be actively managed.

Even with the loss of long-term macro anchors, we believe mega forces are durable drivers of returns – and are findinganchorsinmegaforces, our third theme. Mega forces don’t provide a clear handle on the growth and inflation outlook, unlike macro anchors, and don’t map into broad return drivers. Instead, we need to track their evolution across and within asset classes, get granular with themes and constantly adapt to what’s priced in.

Losing long-term macro anchors

As unusual as the first half of this year seemed, we think it is a more acute manifestation of the profound transformation we have been describing for some years now. Geopolitical fragmentation, AI and other mega forces are reshaping the trajectory and makeup of the global economy.

This is not a cyclical adjustment but a structural one that can lead to many very different outcomes. Elevated uncertainty is a given. What matters is getting to grips with it. That starts with identifying this environment’s core features.

Another way to put it: The loss of long-term macro anchors that markets have relied on for decades. Inflation expectations are no longer firmly anchored near 2% targets.

Fiscal discipline is ebbing away. The compensation investors want for holding long-term U.S. Treasuries is rising from suppressed levels. And confidence in institutional anchors – central bank independence and the haven role of U.S. assets – has been shaken.

We think that requires a new approach to risk taking. Before the pandemic, when long-term macro anchors were mostly in place, broad asset class returns tended to converge back toward long-term averages. Stable growth, low inflation and supportive monetary policy made it easier to generate returns via static exposures to broad stock and bond indexes. And when equities sold off, bonds tended to cushion the blow. This has changed. With long-run economic trajectories now ever-evolving, one would expect investors to search data for signals about where things are headed. This is exactly what we’ve seen. Equity returns have become more sensitive to short-term data as investors try to infer what it means about both the near and long term. See the chart.

That does not mean investors should trim risk taking. Two other features of this environment help. First: even with an uncertain outlook, we believe immutable economic laws narrow near-term outcomes. Second: mega forces offer an alternative anchor for returns. See page 8.

With macro anchors weakening, investors can no longer rely on broad asset class returns converging back toward long-term averages.

World can’t change quickly

Market narratives have flip-flopped constantly this year and at times embraced rapid changes to the global financial order. Yet immutable economic laws on trade and debt are constraining U.S. policy shifts – and can help investors navigate near-term uncertainty. We believe we now have more certainty about the

near-term macro outlook than the long – a big change from the past.

One law limiting trade policy: supply chains can’t be rewired quickly without major disruption. Companies can’t just source products and inputs from elsewhere overnight without a halt in activity. We believe that rule was behind the rapid tariff carve-outs — such as exemptions for electronics from China — and why the U.S. and China soon restarted trade talks.

One goal driving U.S. trade policy is the administration’s wish to fix global imbalances: The U.S. has long been a net importer, resulting in persistent current account deficits. But the causes of these imbalances are complex.

Trade flows reflect deeper forces – like the savings-investment gap – rooted in exchange rates, fiscal policy, demographics and global capital flows, not just trade policy.

That links to a second law on debt:

U.S. debt sustainability relies on big, steady funding by foreign investors, who hold about a quarter of it. See chart. Any falloff in foreign demand for Treasuries could spike yields and make borrowing costs so high that it forces a policy response. We think the tariff pause soon after the April 2 announcement was likely partly due to the yield spike. We see a fragile equilibrium – elevated debt, sticky inflation and higher interest rates – making U.S. Treasuries vulnerable to investors seeing them as riskier. In March, we estimated the U.S. deficit- to-GDP ratio could reach 5-7% by 2030, based on external forecasts of the impact of proposed trade, fiscal and immigration policy. Moody’s has since cut the U.S. top-notch credit rating and Congress is considering a budget bill that we think could push it to 6-7.5%. We think this will keep that rule on debt front and center.

Immutable economic laws limit the range of near-term outcomes. Upending decades-old trade and capital structures takes time, no matter policy intent.

Investing in the here and now

These immutable laws limit how fast global trade and capital markets can evolve. Longer term, with macro anchors lost, no one knows where the global economy is ultimately headed. That’s why, for now, we invest in the here and now – and lean more on our tactical six- to 12- month horizon. It’s also why we stay positive on risk taking and overweight U.S. equities. We also stand ready to lean against sharp market swings if we think markets are overreacting to short-term news and pricing in big, fast changes to the global order.

Since 2000, European equities have had many periods of outperformance over U.S. equities – but they have become increasingly rare. See the shaded areas in the chart. Yet those rallies never spurred questions about the role of U.S. assets as we’ve seen this year. We think the current economic setup still supports U.S. outperformance. We see scope for overall corporate earnings to stay solid even if U.S. growth is dented by tariff-induced disruptions and corporate caution.

What would spur a change in our relative preference? Europe taking bigger steps to address structural reforms. See page 11. For now, we don’t see Europe besting U.S. growth, especially given its dependency on the U.S. On corporate earnings, the U.S. still leads on profit margins and return on equity, warranting higher valuations. That’s why the recent European stock outperformance has already faded. A sharp increase in expected policy rates is a risk to our positive view, but markets already reflect our higher-for-longer policy rate view. And investors demanding a higher term premium in government bonds makes equities and credit relatively more attractive.

We stay selective in European equities with sectors like financials. We prefer euro area government bonds and credit over the U.S. The potential for a further U.S. dollar retreat and brighter emerging market (EM) growth outlook make local currency EM bonds more attractive in a whole portfolio context, in our view.

Investment implications

  • Near-term outcomes are clearer than the long term — a reversal of usual investing logic — so we put more emphasis on tactical views.
  • We are broadly risk on, stay overweight U.S. equities and see plentiful alpha opportunities.

Taking risk with no macro anchor

This year, we see persistent U.S. inflation pressures from rising geopolitical fragmentation, slowing immigration – and eventually, the direct impact of tariffs not yet visible in the CPI. Wage growth is still too high for inflation to fall back to the Fed’s 2% target, and inflation is still volatile. We expect the Federal Reserve to hold rates steady as it waits for more clarity on the tariff impact on growth. We think

The chart shows how top-performing portfolio managers have delivered more alpha since 2020. The median manager is seeing a bigger drag on returns from static factor exposures. That underscores why we think macro risk needs to be managed.

This alpha can be generated by other types of risk taking that can be more rewarding in this environment. One is mega forces – see page 8.

Taking relative risk or postioning for prices of different securities to converge or diverge, is a well- established one. We categorize some others to be more deliberate about taking risk. They include potential deregulation or regulatory changes that could offer alpha opportunities – see the evolving U.S. approach to cryptocurrencies. Positioning risk is about recognizing when markets are crowded, while liquidity risk is about opportunities to provide it during periods of stress. Sharp narrative swings mean we can see near-term selloffs running counter to our tactical views – and view those as opportunities to deploy risk.

Investment implications

  • We think this is an exceptional environment for alpha. But macro risk should be deliberately managed – or neutralized.
  • Other types of risk taking that can generate alpha include relative value and liquidity.

Finding anchors in mega forces

Even with weaker long-term macro anchors, we can have conviction that mega forces will be durable drivers of returns. The importance of our mega forces framework is clear given how this year’s big investment themes have been shaped by them, from AI and the energy transition to geopolitical fragmentation and the future of finance. Yet we don’t know the end state of the economic transformation these mega forces are driving. That’s why they require constant tracking across and within asset classes.

The transformation is grounded in surging capital spending. Big tech firms — the “hyperscalers” powering AI’s expansion — are still boosting spending, according to our compilation of estimates. See the chart. Capital spending and infrastructure is at the heart of many mega forces. But big capital spending does not necessarily result in big returns, as we have seen with the energy transition and security theme, highlighting how assessing what’s in the price is key. See page 10 for more on AI.

A clear example of intersecting mega forces: surging demand from technology is running up against the hard limits of power generation and grid capacity. Private markets are playing a growing role in funding infrastructure, energy systems and the AI buildout. As they grow, private and public markets are becoming more complementary — prompting new approaches to blending them in portfolios. See page 13 for more.

Geopolitical fragmentation adds another layer: Governments are steering capital with industrial policy, tightening controls on critical tech and pushing for energy security. These pressures are reinforcing mega force linkages, with national security concerns helping sustain and drive demand for all forms of power – including nuclear. We explore these cross-currents more fully on the following pages.

We are tracking these shifts through policy developments, capex pipelines and supply chain movements. That helps us to quickly adjust investment views.

Investment implications

  • We see mega forces as a durable driver of

returns in this regime.

  • We favor exposures tied to the AI buildout, rising power demand and infrastructure – and see private capital taking a key role in funding.

Deepening fragmentation

The mega force of geopolitical fragmentation is rapidly evolving. Shocks like the pandemic, the Ukraine war and rising trade tensions have deepened fragmentation over the last decade. That has sped up this year as the new U.S. administration has moved quickly to reorder U.S. geopolitical and economic relationships with the rest of the world. Volatility is high as an end to the Ukraine war appears far off and fresh conflict has arisen in the Middle East.

What is the impact of geopolitical fragmentation in Europe? Defense and industrial competitiveness are back in focus. The Ukraine war had already exposed structural weaknesses in industry and logistics. Now, a shifting U.S. security posture is driving up defense spending in the European Union (EU). After undershooting for over 20 years, it has hit its target of 2% of GDP. See the chart. That is set to keep rising after many NATO members committed to a target of 5% of GDP – 3.5% to defense and 1.5% to defense infrastructure.

Technology is also now a key part of national security. Europe has so far prioritized regulation over industrial support. That may shift. The U.S. sees AI as a “force multiplier,” a tool to enhance intelligence, military capability and autonomous systems.

On trade, the U.S. and EU are yet to reach a trade deal. And Chinese competition is challenging some of Europe’s core sectors, notably autos. Yet, Europe’s competitiveness is an issue that runs deeper than this mega force. The Draghi report last year called for faster decision-making – so far difficult with 27 member states – as well as coordinated industrial policy and large-scale investment. Jean Monnet may be right again that “Europe will be forged in crisis.”

Rebuilding industrial capacity to plug shortfalls in defense is a strategic priority for Europe.” - Donilon Chairman, BlackRock Investment Institute

Taking stock of AI’s phases

We’ve laid out how we see the AI mega force evolving over three phases. Phase 1 is the buildout – the race to build AI’s infrastructure needs. Phase 2 is adoption – with AI packaged into different apps and software. Phase 3 is transformation – where AI adoption potentially boosts productivity, creating new business models and industries.

The AI buildout powers ahead. The competition among the mega cap tech hyperscalers rushing to

The hyperscalers are sticking with increased capital spending – and being rewarded for it. We still like the hardware makers and those sectors benefitting from the buildout, including the utility sector. Utilities and companies linked to AI power demand are seeing many more mentions of AI than the S&P 500 average. See the chart.

Yet for those with insights into potential individual company winners in the AI race, an alpha-seeking approach is better suited. AI intersects with multiple mega forces, including the energy transition, geopolitical fragmentation and the healthcare needs of aging societies.

"Uneven monetization of AI tools is creating mispricings across the value chain." - Rowan Palmour Research analyst, BlackRock Fundamental Equities.

U.S. assets still core

The joint drop in U.S. stocks, bonds and the dollar in April spurred questions about the long-term appeal of U.S. assets. We don’t think those concerns are justified. We still see U.S. assets as core to portfolios – but with greater selectivity – and see opportunities across regions and asset classes.

U.S. stocks have outperformed for a decade, driven by economic resilience, tech leadership and

Europe may offer upside as fiscal support lifts revenues in aerospace, defense and financials. Yet as we noted earlier, we think Europe needs reforms to be able to outperform on a sustained basis.

One question is whether the weak U.S. net international investment position (NIIP) – the difference between its international liabilities and its assets – is a threat to U.S. equity valuations. Yet the steady of US NIIP need not be a problem, in our view. It reflects a historically strong dollar and ongoing global demand for U.S. risk assets – rather than worsening contributions from current account deficits. Rising fiscal deficits could pose a bigger challenge, we think.

U.S. dollar’s dominant role

The U.S. dollar’s 10% slide against major currencies this year has raised questions about its historical global haven and reserve role. We think it would take a lot – and some time – to change the dollar’s role as the backbone in the global financial system.

It took decades and world wars for the dollar to replace sterling and gold as the top reserve asset. The dollar’s central role has been reinforced since the global financial crisis. The dollar appears on one side of nearly 90% of all foreign exchange (FX) transactions. See the chart.
